Q: For a woman with a regular cycle, fertilization usually occurs within 12 hours of ovulation. However, pregnancy can still result if sexual intercourse occurred up to how many days prior to ovulation?
EXP: The text states that "sperm deposited in the reproductive tract up to 6 days prior to ovulation can survive to fertilize oocytes."

Q: While most infants who are "small for gestational age" (SGA) may be constitutionally small but healthy, those with "intrauterine growth restriction" (IUGR) are at increased risk for complications. Which of the following is listed as a potential cause of IUGR?

Options.D: Correct establish of the L-R axis
Options.E: Increased physical activity during the third trimester

EXP: The text lists "chromosomal abnormalities" as one of the causative factors for intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR).

Q: A 39-year-old pregnant woman is informed that her age increases the risk of chromosomal abnormalities in the fetus. According to the text, which of the following is an invasive procedure typically offered to high-risk patients to obtain a definitive diagnosis of major chromosomal alterations?

Options.C: Noninvasive prenatal screening (NIPS) using cell-free DNA

EXP: The text states that invasive prenatal diagnostic tests such as amniocentesis are reserved for high-risk pregnancies, including women 35 years and older. Amniocentesis allows for metaphase karyotyping to identify major chromosomal alterations like trisomies.

Q: Advanced paternal age has been identified as a significant factor in the inheritance of genetic conditions. According to the text, what is the dominant factor in determining the number of de novo mutations that appear in a child?

Options.A: The age of the mother at the time of conception
Options.C: The age of the father at the time of reproduction
Options.D: The use of assisted reproductive technologies like IVF
Options.E: The duration of the second stage of labor

EXP: The text states that men transmit a higher number of mutations to their children than women, and the age of the father is the dominant factor in determining how many de novo mutations appear in the child.

Q: Paternal exposure to environmental toxins can impact embryonic development through male-mediated teratogenesis. Which of the following is a mechanism mentioned in the text by which a father can transmit toxicity to the fetus or mother?

Options.A: Transmission of mitochondria through the sperm head
Options.B: Contamination from chemicals brought home on work clothes
Options.C: Induction of epigenetic changes in the maternal uterus
Options.D: Direct injection of toxins into the amniotic sac during intercourse

EXP: The text notes that transmission of paternally mediated toxicity is possible through seminal fluid and from household contamination from chemicals brought home on work clothes by the father.

Q: Assisted reproductive technology (ART) has become a common treatment for infertility. According to the text, which specific procedure is associated with even higher rates of birth defects than standard in vitro fertilization?
EXP: The text indicates that in vitro fertilization (IVF) techniques are associated with an increase in birth defects and that these rates are higher with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) procedures.

Q: In comparing the genetic contributions and risks between oogenesis and spermatogenesis, which of the following statements regarding mutations is supported by the text?

Options.A: Females transmit a higher number of mutations to their offspring than males.
Options.B: Male germ cells are immune to the effects of radiation and ethylnitrosourea.
Options.C: Men transmit a higher number of mutations to their children than women.
Options.D: De novo mutations are exclusively caused by maternal nutritional deficiencies.
Options.E: Advanced maternal age is the only relevant factor for autosomal dominant mutations.

EXP: The text explicitly states that men transmit a higher number of mutations to their children than women, and that advanced paternal age is a factor for new autosomal dominant mutations.
